title: Bienenstich
subtitle: German bee sting cake with caramelised almond topping and vanilla cream
excerpt: Bienenstich is a German yeast cake baked in a round tin, topped with caramelised almonds that set to a crisp honeyed crust during baking. Split and filled with a thick vanilla custard cream after cooling. The crunch of the almond top against the soft dough and cold cream is the whole point.
sourceNotes: Bienenstich (German, literally bee sting) is a traditional German baked cake with documented roots in the Rhineland going back to at least the nineteenth century. The name is variously explained, but the recipe is consistent: a yeasted dough topped with a hot caramelised almond and butter mixture that bakes to a crisp golden crust. The filling is typically a vanilla pastry cream or a creme diplomat. This recipe follows the established German domestic tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry cream can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. The dough can be proved overnight in the fridge. The filled cake is best eaten on the day it is assembled.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the cake bakes in 22 to 25 minutes until the almond topping is deep amber

glossaryTerms.definition: The topping is made by bringing butter, sugar, honey and cream to a gentle boil until the sugar dissolves, then stirring in flaked almonds. The mixture is poured directly over the proved dough before baking. It spreads and caramelises in the oven, forming a crisp, honeyed crust that holds the almonds in a brittle toffee-like layer.

content.text: Bienenstich arrives at the table as a complete thing: a soft yeasted cake, a crisp
, and a cold vanilla cream filling that melts against both. It takes two hours from start to finish, but most of that is waiting.

Combine 350 g flour, 5 g yeast, 50 g sugar and 5 g salt in the bowl of a stand mixer fitted with the dough hook. Add 150 ml warm milk, 1 egg and 60 g softened butter. Mix on low for 2 minutes, then on medium for 6 minutes until the dough is smooth and slightly tacky. Shape into a ball, return to the bowl, cover and prove for 1 hour until doubled.
Grease a 23 cm round cake tin. Turn out the proved dough, knock back and press into the tin in an even layer. Cover and prove for 30 minutes until noticeably puffed.
Make the almond topping and bake

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Melt 80 g butter in a small saucepan with 80 g caster sugar, 2 tbsp honey and 2 tbsp double cream. Bring to a gentle boil, stirring, for 2 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in 150 g flaked almonds. Spoon the hot topping evenly over the proved dough in the tin. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the almond topping is deep amber and the cake is set. The top should look like caramelised brittle. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then remove and cool completely on a rack.

Whisk 4 egg yolks, 100 g caster sugar, 40 g cornflour and 1 tsp vanilla extract together in a bowl until smooth. Heat 500 ml whole milk in a saucepan until just below a boil. Pour a third of the hot milk into the egg mixture, whisking constantly, then pour everything back into the pan. Cook over medium heat, stirring continuously, until the mixture thickens and begins to bubble, about 3 to 4 minutes. Pour into a shallow bowl, press cling film onto the surface and refrigerate until cold, at least 30 minutes. Once cold, whip 200 ml double cream to soft peaks and fold into the cold custard.

Using a long serrated knife, cut the cooled cake horizontally through the middle. Lift the top half off carefully (the almond crust is brittle; use a board for support). Spread the vanilla cream thickly over the base. Replace the almond-crusted top half. Refrigerate for 20 minutes to let the cream firm slightly before cutting.

items.problem: The almond topping sinks into the dough during baking
items.solution: The topping was too hot and loose when added, or the dough was under-proved. Make sure the topping has had 2 minutes at a gentle boil so it thickens slightly before adding the almonds. The proved dough should feel noticeably bouncy before the topping goes on.
The custard was not stirred continuously when it thickened. Keep whisking as soon as the mixture starts to coat the spoon. If lumps appear, pour through a sieve immediately while still hot.
